items.body:
Your mental model is basically correct:

  1. build the status line
  2. build the headers
  3. finish the header section
  4. write the body

With BaseHTTPRequestHandler, those steps are split across different methods.

send_response() does not mean “send the whole response body now”. According to the Python docs, headers are stored in an internal buffer, and this changed in Python 3.3:[web:408]

“Headers are stored to an internal buffer and end_headers() needs to be called explicitly.”[web:408]

So in practice:

  • send_response(200) adds the status line (HTTP/1.0 200 OK) to the internal headers buffer and also adds default headers like Server and Date.[web:408]
  • send_header(...) adds more header lines to that same buffer.[web:408]
  • end_headers() writes the terminating blank line and flushes the buffered status line + headers to the client.[web:408][web:421]
  • self.wfile.write(...) writes the response body bytes after the headers have been finished.[web:417]

So the correct way to think about it is:

send_response(...)
send_header(...)
send_header(...)
end_headers()
wfile.write(...)

Thanks, this clears it up.

That makes the flow much easier to understand before moving to frameworks like FastAPI.
